macro_segments.title: Introduction and Review of Chazaka Principles

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and transition to new section of Mishnah
Chazaka defined: three years day-by-day for ongoing use (homes, irrigated fields)

macro_segments.title: Evidence of Ownership Beyond Three-Year Possession: Basket of Fruit Case

micro_segments.title: Rav Abba: Witnessing owner carrying fruit creates immediate presumption of ownership
Rav Zevid: Exception when owner claims fruit-bearer was sharecropper, not purchaser
Gemara clarifies: sharecropper defense only works within three years
Discussion of Michah (formal protest) and its necessity after three years to protect interests

macro_segments.title: Mechah (Protest) as Institution for Protecting Interests in Legitimate Land Use

micro_segments.title: Rav Ashi to Rav Kahana: If genuinely a sharecropper, how does owner protect his interests?
Gemara explains mechah can be made to distinguish legitimate sharecropper from owner
Concern raised: mechah for sharecropper less effective than for thieves; does not protect buyer sufficiently
Mashkanta desura (collateralized loan) case demonstrates need for mechah even in legitimate arrangements
Conclusion: mechah protects creditor/owner; buyer must be careful with documents

macro_segments.title: Acquisition Through Non-Jew: Chazaka Does Not Apply

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda: If buyer's chain includes non-Jew, chazaka claim fails
Reason: non-Jews not subject to chazaka; position of power allows seizure without protest
Rav Yosef challenges: why does buyer's testimony about non-Jew's claim help if non-Jew's own claim fails?
Rava: Buyer believed only if he has direct knowledge of non-Jew's acquisition (saw document or witnessed sale)
Application of miggo: since buyer could claim direct purchase from original owner, his claim of indirect purchase through non-Jew is credible

macro_segments.title: Specific Produce and Land Cases Where Chazaka Does Not Apply

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda: Bold claim without prior action (harvesting palm fruit) is believed without three-year possession
Buffer zone (gudi d'arudai): produce from edge of field not chazaka because owner abandons it to animals
Orlah (first-fruit): eating forbidden fruit is not chazaka; owner has no interest in it
Shevi'it (sabbatical) and kilayim (mixed species): not chazaka; owner abandons interest or all have rights
Sachis (fodder): harvesting only regrowth, not full crop, shows furtive behavior, not ownership
Rava: In valleys where fodder cultivation is normal, sachis harvest does count as chazaka
Tafticha (cracked, sun-baked land): no chazaka; nothing valuable grows; owner will not protest
Apei kura (land with one-to-one seed-to-yield ratio): no profit, no chazaka; owner has reason not to protest

macro_segments.title: Chazaka Among Those in Power: Resh Galuta and Government Officials

micro_segments.title: Land held by or through resh galuta (exilarch) and his household: no chazaka
Reason: those in power can seize land; minority Jews cannot safely protest, so silence is not acquiescence
Reciprocal rule: Jews possessing resh galuta's land also lack chazaka; he can reclaim by force
Rashbam explains: silence not meaningful acquiescence when powerful party can simply take back by force

macro_segments.title: Chazaka for Animals and Moveable Property Versus Land

micro_segments.title: Slaves and moving things: immediate physical possession does not equal ownership (unlike normal movables)
Reish Lakish: flocks (godrot tzon) cannot be acquired by possession; they roam on their own volition
Rava: slaves and roaming animals require three-year chazaka after all (parallel to land)
Rationale: living creatures with independent movement; ownership cannot be proven by mere possession
Infant slave exception: immediate chazaka if in cradle; cannot roam, so physical possession proves ownership
Mother will not abandon child; therefore possession by someone else establishes acquisition from mother or original owner

macro_segments.title: Goats Case: Chazaka Through Physical Seizure and Miggo Argument

micro_segments.title: Goats eat barley in field; owner seizes goats to prove damage and claim compensation
Rav Yehuda's father: credible up to the value of the goats themselves
Miggo applied: claimant could say 'I bought the goats'; therefore believed to claim their full value in damages
Challenge: Reish Lakish says flocks have no chazaka; how does immediate seizure work here?
Resolution: goats differ from sheep; constantly supervised by shepherd, not left to roam freely
Caveat: morning and evening gaps when not supervised; location (Nehardea) affects supervision continuity

macro_segments.title: Rain-Fed Fields and Partial-Year Chazaka: Rabbi Yishmael versus Rabbi Akiva

micro_segments.title: For rain-fed fields (sde habaal), not year-round use: three years can be represented by partial years
Rabbi Yishmael: three months end of year one, full middle year, three months beginning of year three
Rabbi Akiva: one month end of year one, full middle year, one month beginning of year three
Multiple harvests in one year (grapes, dates, figs) count as three-year chazaka within single calendar year
Possible difference: whether plowing (nir) alone constitutes chazaka; Yishmael requires substantial fruit

macro_segments.title: Plowing (Nir) as Chazaka: Dispute and Resolution

micro_segments.title: Tannaitic dispute: nir (plowing/tilling) is not chazaka; some opinions (Rebbe Acha) say it is
Pattern rule: nir for one year + zara (planting) for two years, or vice versa—not chazaka
Rebbe Acha: nir within context of mixed tilling and planting shows owner behavior; is chazaka
Majority: nir alone insufficient; requires harvest to give context and show real ownership
Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak invokes Rav, Shmuel, Rabbi Yishmael, Rabbi Akiva: nir does not work
Proof from Mishnah: if nir worked, one month or three months (Yishmael/Akiva) would be unnecessary
Rav Yehuda citing Rav: three full years needed; nir excluded from miyon (restrictive phrase 'mi-yom l'yom')
Shmuel holds: three full harvests needed, not necessarily three calendar years
Difference applied: young palm tree yields multiple harvests per year; affects when chazaka complete
Conclusion pending: does mechanic of chazaka require three full calendar years or three full harvests? (To be continued)

topical_tags.term: Three years day-by-day (shalosh shanim yom l'yom)
Miggo (if you could have made stronger claim, weaker one believed)
Flocks/Godrot tzon (roaming animals without immediate chazaka)
Resh galuta (exilarch with power-based exemption from chazaka)
Rain-fed field (sde habaal with seasonal cycles)
Rabbi Yishmael (tannaitic disputant on partial years and nir)
Rabbi Akiva (tannaitic disputant on partial years and nir)
Rav Abba (Amora on basket of fruit as immediate proof)
Rav Kahana (Amora addressed by Rav Ashi)
Reish Lakish (Amora on flocks and chazaka)
Rava (Amora on slave chazaka and non-Jew chain)
Rav Yosef (Amora challenging non-Jew testimony logic)
Rebbe Acha (Tannaim/Amora supporting nir as chazaka)
Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak (Amora rejecting nir-only chazaka)
Shmuel (foundational Amora; three harvests vs. three years)
Rashbam (medieval commentator on force and silence)
